summary:
Fell's Cave is a prehistoric rock shelter at the southern tip of South America, in the Magallanes Region of Chilean Patagonia near the town of Puerto Natales, excavated in the 1930s and subsequently re-investigated. The cave contains archaeological materials dated to approximately 10,000–8,000 BCE that document some of the earliest known human occupation of southern Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego — the southernmost inhabited region on earth.

The earliest cultural layer at Fell's Cave (Fell 1) contains Fishtail points — a distinctive style of bifacially flaked projectile points with a characteristic fishtail base — alongside the remains of now-extinct Pleistocene megafauna including the horse (Hippidion), native American camel (Lama gracilis), and ground sloth (Mylodon). This association of early human hunters with now-extinct Pleistocene fauna in the extreme south of the continent is one of the clearest demonstrations that humans reached the tip of South America while megafauna were still present, supporting the role of human hunting in their eventual [[event:1289|extinction]]. Fell's Cave is one of the defining early sites for understanding the rapid peopling of South America during the late Pleistocene and early Holocene.
